title: Bayon Temple Guide - The Smiling Faces of Angkor - Just Siem Reap
highlights: smiling faces looking down on you from the 37 towers. Located in the centre of Angkor Thom, it was once the state temple for Jayavarman VII. The Buddhist temple  ...    No visit to Angkor is complete without seeing the famous faces of Bayon temple. You’ll see them everywhere around town,  ...    You can explore the three tiers, gaze up to the faces looking back down at you, and imagine what it would have looked  ...    these plans were changed after construction had started. There were 49 towers when Bayon was first built, but only 37  ...    The towers feature around 200 faces. There is some discussion about who these faces belong to. Firstly, they could belong to King Jayavarman VII who like most Angkor kings, considered himself a devaraja or god king. Alternatively, the faces are said to belong to Avalokitesvara – a bodhisattva who represents all Buddhas. The main central tower used to feature a seated Buddha who was protected by Mucal

title: What Does Bayon Temple Represent? The Face-Filled Mystery of ...
highlights: Jayavarman VII built this amazing place. The stone faces are really big – as tall as a giraffe! Some faces smile, others look serious. All of them seem to follow you with their eyes. What does Bayon Temple represent? It shows the king’s power and his Buddhist faith. You’ll want to bring your camera when you visit.  ...    Bayon Temple has 216 huge faces carved in stone. They’re about 13 feet tall! Four faces sit on each tower, looking north, south, east, and west. Many people feel like the eyes follow them around. These faces stand for a Buddhist being named Avalokitesvara. He helps people who are sad or in trouble. What does Bayon  ...    Hindu ways. All those faces stand for Avalokitesvara, who Buddhists believe helps anyone who needs it. The faces look at all four directions because they want to see everyone. title: The Bayon – The Temple of Faces | theraulaz.ch
highlights:
These faces are believed to represent King Jayavarman VII himself, or a fusion of the king and the bodhisattva Avalokiteshvara.  ...    earlier Hindu temples, the Bayon is a Mahayana Buddhist sanctuary, dedicated to Lokeshvara, the bodhisattva of  ...    These smiling faces, with half-closed eyes, embody transcendent serenity. Their meaning remains debated: - Expression of the king’s benevolent watchfulness over the cardinal directions. - Idealized portrait of Jayavarman VII, deified through the bodhisattva. - Representations of Lokeshvara, embodiment of universal compassion.  ...    ## Religious Symbolism

The Bayon expresses a Buddhist cosmology centered on the king: - The faces’ orientation implies a divine presence in all directions, an omniscient and benevolent power. - The temple embodies a mandala of a balanced universe, with the king at its center. - The

# The Bayon: A temple with many faces byDr. Melody Rod-ari
Artwork DetailsDownward pointing arrow  ...    The mystery over the temple’s name contributes to the enigmatic face-towers that define the temple’s architecture. Since the Bayon’s rediscovery by French scholars and explorers in the 19th century, the identity of the smiling figures that greet visitors to the temple and to the city of Angkor Thom has been debated. Do they depict Buddhist or Hindu deities, or do they depict King Jayavarman VII smiling over his empire? Inscriptions from the temple along with its  ... Mottl, CC BY-SA 4.0)
